retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Resistance Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oral appliances offer an effective alternative for patients with UARS who are intolerant of CPAP or decline surgical intervention (see Image . Custom Oral Appliance for Upper Airway Resistance Syndrome). [22] These devices advance the mandible and soft tissue anteriorly, increasing the airway space posterior to the tongue. Long-term use, such as for 1.5 years, has been associated with reductions in stress-related symptoms, as measured by the Inventory of Stress Symptoms. [23] Additional oral appliance options include tongue-retaining devices, soft palate lifters, and tongue stabilizers.

retrieved_docs.title: Velopharyngeal Insufficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Palatal muscle repositioning maneuvers lengthen or readjust the orientation of palatal muscles. The Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty was originally used for primary cleft palate repair but had the added benefit of lengthening the palate by reorienting the levator veli palatini muscles transversely. [29] [30] [31] It also has a lower risk of causing obstructive sleep apnea compared to the sphincter pharyngoplasty and pharyngeal flap. [32] Intravelar veloplasty involves realigning the levator veli palatini for cleft palate patients who underwent inadequate muscle correction after their primary palate repair. The soft palate is opened along the midline with meticulous dissection and realignment of the abnormally inserted muscles. [4] [20] Buccal myomucosal flaps raised from the inner aspect of the cheek have also allowed the lengthening of the velum and retroposition of palate muscles. [33] [34] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Ramsay Hunt Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, the most apparent feature on physical examination will be unilateral hemifacial palsy, which will be complete in close to 50% of patients. [14] Facial analysis may reveal the absence of transverse forehead rhytides, brow ptosis, lagophthalmos, ectropion, effacement of the nasolabial fold, the collapse of the nasal valve, malposition of the oral commissure inferiorly, and absence of platysmal banding on the affected side. The philtrum and nasal base will also likely shift away from the paralyzed side. Examination of a facial paralysis patient should be performed in three distinct passes. The first should look for asymmetry at rest, evaluating the upper third of the face, followed by the middle, and then the lower face and neck. The second should assess each of the major extratemporal branches of the facial nerve in sequence: frontal by brow elevation, zygomatic by gentle and forceful eye closure, buccal by smiling, marginal mandibular by depression of the lower lip, and cervical by platysmal contraction. Evaluating each of these movements also makes grading and documentation using the House-Brackmann scale straightforward. In the House-Brackmann system, a grade I represents normal facial function, grade II slight asymmetry with movement but complete eye closure with gentle effort, grade III more asymmetry with movement but complete eye closure with maximal effort only, grade IV still greater asymmetry with movement and inability to close the eye completely, grade V gross resting asymmetry and inability to close the eye, and grade VI demonstrates no movement at all. [29] Numerous other clinical facial nerve grading systems are available, but the House-Brackmann scale remains the most widely employed due to its ease of use. [30] [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Char Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Typical facial features include a depressed nasal bridge and broad flat nasal tip, widely spaced eyes, down-slanted palpebral fissures, mild ptosis, a short philtrum with prominent philtrum ridges with an upward pointing vermilion border resulting in a triangular mouth and thickened (patulous) everted lips. [3] The most commonly identified cardiac anomaly is PDA. [1] Less common findings include other types of congenital heart defects, other hand and foot anomalies, hypodontia, hearing loss, myopia and/or strabismus, polythelia, parasomnia, craniosynostosis (involving either the metopic or sagittal suture), and short stature. [1] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Crouzon Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Dental abnormalities: Malocclusion, congestion, and a high-arched palate are common in these patients, complicating speech development and feeding. [72]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment / Management -- Straight-line repair and variations
retrieved_docs.content: The soft palate portion involves elevating the nasal mucosal surface off of the palatal sling muscles, which in the modern era are detached from their aberrant insertions into the posterior hard palate and reapproximated to form a palatal sling, and then the oral mucosal layer is closed as a distinct layer (see Image . Von Langenbeck Palatoplasty Diagram). [44] This basic, multilayered, straight-line closure was adopted in 1859 by von Langenbeck, who described lateral relaxing incisions, essentially creating pedicled flaps of both oral palatal mucosal layers. The von Langenbeck technique significantly decreased failure and fistula rate by decreasing tension on the surgical closure and allowed the repair of much wider palatal clefts. [45] The von Langenbeck technique is still employed today, particularly in the NAM setting where clefts may be narrow. This technique does not lengthen the palate and is most useful when the soft palate already makes adequate contact with the posterior pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Although these methods provide valuable insight into achieving an ideal chin position concerning the lips, face, and neck, only a few account for the crucial relationship between the nose and chin. This relationship is vital to acknowledge, as they directly influence each other's relative appearance in space. As a general rule, a more projected nose tends to make the chin appear less projected, and vice versa. An essential and ideal harmonious balance exists between the projection of the chin and the nose. Notably, all the mentioned analysis methods rely on standardized photography techniques, with four of them utilizing the Frankfort horizontal line—a well-established anatomical reference extending from the superior border of the external auditory canal to the inferior orbital rim. [5] Furthermore, radiographic evaluation, including panoramic radiographs and/or lateral and anterior-posterior (AP) radiographs, may be beneficial for addressing more complex deformities such as vertical height excess, orthognathic abnormalities, and transverse asymmetry, commonly observed in conditions such as hemifacial/craniofacial microsomia, oculoauricular vertebral (OAV) syndrome, or isolated anatomical components.

retrieved_docs.title: Velopharyngeal Insufficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cephalometrics uses serial X-rays from multiple views to study velopharyngeal anatomic relationships during sustained speech. The anterior insertion of the levator veli palatini muscle into the soft palate is assessed, with more anterior insertion (seen in occult cleft palates) resulting in decreased soft tissue stretch and a velopharyngeal gap during speech. Abnormalities in the cervical spine or skull base (platybasia) can contribute to nasopharyngeal disproportion. Cephalometrics is a quantifiable and reliable evaluation for treatment planning in children with velocardiofacial syndrome and isolated cleft palates. [3] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Consideration in Pierre Robin Sequence -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: After initial stabilization of the patient, procedures such as tongue lip adhesion and mandibular distraction osteogenesis can correct glossoptosis, lengthen the mandible and relieve glossoptosis. Also, the above techniques may need to be performed in conservatively managed PRS who fail to achieve an adequate catch-up growth. [10] [11] Patients often require palatoplasty to correct the palatal defect, fix feeding difficulties, and facilitate normal speech development.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palate -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Repeated bouts of upper airway obstruction cause obstructive sleep apnea during the deep stages of sleep as a result of the narrowing/collapse of the respiratory passages. Patients with obstructive sleep apnea tend to be overweight with associated peripharyngeal fat infiltration and/or increased size of the tongue and soft palate. These anatomic abnormalities predispose patients to obstructive sleep apnea because they decrease the cross-sectional area of the upper airway. An increased neck circumference corresponds to increased peripharyngeal fat infiltration, specifically lining the airway and at the base of the tongue. Neck circumference should be measured when obstructive sleep apnea is suspected. A neck circumference greater than seventeen inches in men and greater than sixteen inches in women increases the risk for obstructive sleep apnea. One local airway factor thought to be a causative agent of obstructive sleep apnea is an elongated soft palate. A distal palatal segment length greater than fifteen millimeters has shown to increase the risk of sleep apnea dramatically. Initial treatment of sleep apnea is primarily nonsurgical and consists of positive airway pressure therapy, most commonly in the form of CPAP. Historically, many of the surgical techniques used to correct sleep apnea in adults were sub-optimal, and palatopharyngoplasty techniques have evolved from primarily excisional methods to those that reposition and reconstruct the palate. Examples of these more advanced surgical techniques include the expansion sphincter-pharyngoplasty, lateral pharyngoplasty, relocation pharyngoplasty, and uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP). [1] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Crouzon Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The underdevelopment of the maxilla and zygomatic arches causes midface hypoplasia, a defining characteristic of Crouzon syndrome. [4] This anomaly leads to aesthetic concerns and contributes to functional impairments, such as malocclusion and upper airway obstruction. [5] Proptosis, hypertelorism, and vision impairment result from orbital dysmorphology, which leads to exposure keratopathy and optic atrophy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tensor Veli Palatini Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Because of TVP’s minor role in the palatal function, the TVP is theorized to be dysfunctional in a patient with cleft palates. Results from a recent study found that in these patients with cleft palates, the TVP is positioned more medially, as well as has a decrease in the volume of the muscle and the length. [2] This positioning may be important in the future to aid in diagnosing more mild cleft palate deformities.

retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific abnormal fetal presentations are organized under generic presentation terms, which in turn are subsumed by the overarching classification of malposition; malpresentation, a key concept for understanding and managing unstable fetal positions during obstetric care.

This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

The Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين” (prolapsed fetal arm) is identified as the parent label for the prolapsed arm presentation, while the phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين” (instability of fetal position) is associated with the “has parent” relationship for malposition; malpresentation, reinforcing the concept that malposition reflects an unstable fetal orientation. In addition, the broader concept “Fetal presentation finding” is linked to malposition; malpresentation through a “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 963
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is an instance of the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that this fetal presentation can occur regardless of the specific clinical episode. The “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” category is classified as a type of “Malposition; malpresentation,” placing it within the overall group of abnormal fetal positions. Within the same “Malposition; malpresentation” group, other specific abnormalities are also classified, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The specific entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is linked to the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” confirming that it refers to a fetal arm that has prolapsed. Additionally, “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is an instance of the broader “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” further illustrating the hierarchical organization of these malpresentation categories. Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy in which face or brow presentations, prolapsed fetal arms, and transverse or oblique presentations are all subtypes of malposition or malpresentation, each linked to more general descriptive categories that are independent of specific episodes of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 736
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal malposition and malpresentation constitute a broad category of abnormal fetal positioning that reflects instability of fetal position (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Within this overarching group, specific fetal presentation findings are classified, including Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, which is identified as a type of malposition; malpresentation. The same classification applies to Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, which is linked to its Arabic parent term تدلّي ذراع الجنين, and to Transverse or oblique presentation of fetus, which is associated with the parent term Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. An additional category, Unspecified malposition or malpresentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, also falls under the broader malposition; malpresentation classification. Collectively, these entities illustrate how various abnormal fetal presentations are organized under the single conceptual framework of malposition; malpresentation, emphasizing their relevance for obstetric assessment and management.
